examples.title: File Operations in C (POSIX)
examples.description: Demonstrates basic file operations: create, open, read, write, seek, close
examples.explanation: This POSIX C code demonstrates essential file system calls. open() with O_CREAT creates a file and returns file descriptor. write() writes data and advances file pointer. lseek() repositions pointer for random access. read() retrieves data. close() releases descriptor and flushes buffers.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for sequential, O(1) for direct access via lseek

examples.title: File Operations in Python
examples.description: High-level file operations demonstrating sequential and random access
examples.code:
# OPEN and WRITE
with open('data.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Line 1\n')
    f.write('Line 2\n')
    f.write('Line 3\n')

# SEQUENTIAL READ
with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    for line in f:  # Sequential access
        print(line.strip())

# RANDOM ACCESS with seek
with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    f.seek(6)  # Position at byte 6
    print(f.read(5))  # Read 5 bytes from position

# DIRECT ACCESS to specific line
with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    for i, line in enumerate(f):
        if i == 2:  # Third line (index 2)
            print(line)
examples.explanation: Python's file objects provide higher-level abstraction. with statement ensures proper closing. seek() positions the file pointer. read() with parameter reads specified bytes. Iteration reads sequentially. For true random access by record, would need struct or fixed-width records.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) sequential, O(1) with seek for positioning
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for basic operations, O(n) when reading entire file

examples.title: Direct Access File Implementation in C
examples.description: Fixed-length record file with direct (random) access using calculated offsets
examples.explanation: This demonstrates direct access method where each record has fixed size. The address is calculated mathematically: offset = record_number × record_size. This allows O(1) access to any record without reading preceding records. Common in database systems.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) - constant time for any record via calculated offset
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only one record in memory at a time

examples.title: Indexed File Access Simulation
examples.description: Simulates indexed access with index file pointing to data blocks
examples.explanation: Simulates indexed file access. First builds an index mapping keys to block addresses. Search operates in two steps: first search the (smaller) index, then directly access the data block using stored address. Index can be searched more efficiently (binary search) than scanning entire file.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n) for index search + O(1) for direct block access = O(log n) total
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing index in memory (can be reduced with multi-level index)
